section.title: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as Stokes
section.p: The generalized Stokes theorem says \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega. The shortest nontrivial case happens when M is an interval. Then the boundary is only two signed endpoints, and \omega is only a function.
That case is not a new theorem. It is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

subsection.title: A changing temperature along a rod
subsection.p: Imagine a thin metal rod lying along the x-axis from x=1 \qquad\text{to}\qquad x=4. The temperature along the rod is T(x)=x^2+2x. Then dT=T'(x)\,dx=(2x+2)\,dx.

The interval I=[1,4] is oriented from left to right. Its boundary is \partial I=\{4\}-\{1\}. So the boundary integral of the 0-form T is
\int_{\partial I}T \amp= T(4)-T(1) \amp= (16+8)-(1+2) \amp= 21.
This is exactly Stokes' theorem: \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega, with M=I, \qquad \omega=T.

subsection.title: A 0-form on an interval
subsection.p: A 0-form is a function. On an interval, \omega=f(x) is a 0-form, and its exterior derivative is the 1-form \boxed{d\omega=df=f'(x)\,dx.}
So the two sides of Stokes become \int_{\partial[a,b]}f and \int_{[a,b]}df.
The first one is an endpoint calculation. The second one is an ordinary integral.
If [a,b] is oriented from a to b, then \partial[a,b]=\{b\}-\{a\}. Therefore \boxed{\int_{\partial[a,b]}f=f(b)-f(a).}
Meanwhile, \boxed{\int_{[a,b]}df=\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx.}
That is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

theorem.title: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as Stokes

subsection.p: The theorem is familiar, but the forms language shows its shape:
subsection.md: \begin{array}{c|c} \text{Stokes language} \amp \text{One-variable language} \\ \hline M=[a,b] \amp \text{the interval of integration}\\[1mm] \partial M=\{b\}-\{a\} \amp \text{right endpoint minus left endpoint}\\[1mm] \omega=f \amp \text{a function}\\[1mm] d\omega=df=f'(x)\,dx \amp \text{the derivative times }dx\\[1mm] \displaystyle\int_{\partial M}\omega \amp f(b)-f(a)\\[3mm] \displaystyle\int_M d\omega \amp \displaystyle\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx \end{array}

subsection.title: The boundary signs are the whole point
subsection.p: The boundary of an interval is not just the set \{a,b\}. It is the signed set \{b\}-\{a\}. Without those signs, the theorem would not know the difference between f(b)-f(a) and f(a)+f(b).

example.title: Reversing the interval reverses the theorem

statement.p: Let f(x)=x^3 on the interval [0,2]. With the usual orientation, \partial[0,2]=\{2\}-\{0\}. So \int_{\partial[0,2]}f=f(2)-f(0)=8. Also \int_{[0,2]}df=\int_0^2 3x^2\,dx=8.

Now reverse the orientation. The same geometric interval is now traveled from 2 to 0. Its boundary is \{0\}-\{2\}. Therefore \int_{\partial(-[0,2])}f=f(0)-f(2)=-8. The integral through the interval also reverses: \int_{-[0,2]}df=\int_2^0 3x^2\,dx=-8. Both sides change sign together.

subsection.p: This is the first lesson about orientation:
subsection.md: \boxed{\text{Reversing orientation reverses both sides of Stokes' theorem.}}

subsection.title: A one-dimensional cancellation picture
subsection.p: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus already contains the cancellation idea behind all versions of Stokes' theorem.

Cut the interval [a,b] into smaller intervals: a=x_0<x_1<x_2<\cdots<x_n=b. On each small interval, [x_{j-1},x_j], the boundary is \{x_j\}-\{x_{j-1}\}. So its boundary contribution is f(x_j)-f(x_{j-1}).

Add all the small boundary contributions:
\amp\bigl(f(x_1)-f(x_0)\bigr)+\bigl(f(x_2)-f(x_1)\bigr)+\cdots+\bigl(f(x_n)-f(x_{n-1})\bigr) \amp\qquad = f(x_n)-f(x_0).
Every interior point appears twice with opposite signs: +f(x_1)-f(x_1), \qquad +f(x_2)-f(x_2), \qquad \ldots Only the outside boundary survives.

figure.caption: The one-dimensional cancellation behind the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

subsection.p: This same cancellation will reappear in the plane. Instead of interior endpoints, we will have interior edges. Instead of df, we will have d(P\,dx+Q\,dy).

subsection.title: Why the smoothness condition is not decorative
subsection.p: The theorem asked for f to be C^1. That is stronger than just being a function with endpoint values. The derivative df must exist and behave well throughout the interval.
Here is what can go wrong.

example.title: A hidden jump breaks the naive formula

statement.p: Away from x=0, the function is constant, so a careless person might say df=0 and conclude \int_{[-1,1]}df=0. That would give 1=0, which is false.

The problem is the jump at x=0. The function is not continuous there, and it is not C^1. In our rules, df is not a valid continuous 1-form on the whole interval. The theorem was never allowed to be used.
The missing smoothness is hiding an internal boundary.

subsection.p: A corner is less dangerous than a jump, but it still needs care.

example.title: A corner can be handled by splitting

statement.p: Let f(x)=|x| on [-1,1]. This function is continuous, but it is not differentiable at x=0. So the C^1 version of the theorem does not apply directly.

But if we split the interval into two smooth pieces, [-1,0] \qquad\text{and}\qquad [0,1], then f(x)=-x \quad\text{on }[-1,0], and f(x)=x \quad\text{on }[0,1]. Thus
\int_{-1}^{0}(-1)\,dx+\int_0^1 1\,dx \amp= -1+1 \amp= 0.
The endpoint difference is also f(1)-f(-1)=1-1=0.
The formula survives after splitting, because the function has no jump. But the single C^1 theorem did not cover the whole interval at once.

subsection.p: This is a useful pattern. Piecewise smooth objects are usually allowed, but the pieces and their boundary signs must be accounted for honestly.

subsection.title: The first Stokes theorem
subsection.p: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is often introduced as a theorem about antiderivatives. In this chapter, it is better to see it as the first boundary theorem.
It says:
subsection.md: \boxed{\text{the total derivative inside an interval equals the signed value on the boundary.}}
subsection.p: The phrase signed value on the boundary is the new part. Once we learn to hear it, the theorem no longer feels like a one-dimensional accident.

The interval has two boundary points. A plane region has a boundary curve. When the same idea moves from endpoints to curves, the derivative inside changes from df to d(P\,dx+Q\,dy). That is where Green's theorem enters the story.
